questions.question: Define Binary Search Tree and state its time complexity for search, insert, and delete operations in average case.
keyPoints: BST is a binary tree where left subtree values < root < right subtree values
Average case search: O(log n)
Average case insert: O(log n)
Average case delete: O(log n)

questions.question: Explain the three cases when deleting a node from a BST.
keyPoints: Case 1: Node is leaf - simply remove it
Case 2: Node has one child - connect parent to child
Case 3: Node has two children - replace with inorder successor/predecessor

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of BST operations? When does this occur?
keyPoints: Worst case: O(n) for all operations
Occurs when tree is completely skewed (degenerate)
Skewed tree behaves like linked list
Height becomes n-1 instead of log n

questions.question: How do you find the minimum and maximum element in a BST? What is the time complexity?
keyPoints: Minimum: traverse left children until NULL (leftmost node)
Maximum: traverse right children until NULL (rightmost node)
Time: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case

questions.question: What is an inorder successor? How is it found in a BST?
keyPoints: Inorder successor is the next node in sorted order
If node has right child: find leftmost node of right subtree
If no right child: go up until we find a parent accessed from left
Used in deletion of node with two children

questions.question: Differentiate between BST and a binary tree.
keyPoints: Binary tree: any tree where each node has max 2 children
BST: binary tree with ordering property (left < root < right)
Inorder traversal of binary tree gives arbitrary order
Inorder traversal of BST always gives sorted order

questions.question: What is the space complexity of recursive BST operations? Explain.
keyPoints: Space complexity is O(h) where h is height
For balanced BST: O(log n)
For skewed BST: O(n)
Represents recursion stack depth

questions.question: Explain the algorithm to search an element in a BST. Write pseudocode and analyze its time and space complexity for best, average, and worst cases.
keyPoints: Start from root, compare with current node
If equal: return found
If less: go to left subtree
If greater: go to right subtree
Continue until found or NULL
Best case: O(1) when element is at root
Average case: O(log n) for balanced tree
Worst case: O(n) for skewed tree
Space: O(h) for recursion stack

questions.question: Insert the following elements into an empty BST in the given order: 50, 30, 70, 20, 40, 60, 80. Draw the resulting tree and list the nodes obtained in inorder, preorder and postorder traversals.
keyPoints: Insert 50 as root
Insert 30 (<50) goes left of 50
Insert 70 (>50) goes right of 50
Continue similarly for 20, 40, 60, 80
Preorder: 50, 30, 20, 40, 70, 60, 80 (root first)
Postorder: 20, 40, 30, 60, 80, 70, 50 (root last)

questions.question: Compare Binary Search Tree with sorted array and hash table for search operation. When should each be preferred?
keyPoints: Sorted array: O(log n) search, O(n) insert/delete
BST: O(log n) search, O(log n) insert/delete (balanced)
Hash table: O(1) average search, O(n) worst case
BST advantages: maintains sorted order, supports range queries
Hash table advantages: faster constant-time operations
Use sorted array: static data, frequent range queries not needed
Use BST: dynamic data, need sorted traversal
Use hash table: exact match searches, speed critical

questions.question: What is a balanced BST? Why is it important? Explain how unbalanced trees affect time complexity.
keyPoints: Balanced BST: height is O(log n) ensuring O(log n) operations
AVL trees, Red-Black trees are self-balancing BSTs
Unbalanced/skewed tree has height O(n)
Operations degrade to O(n) in worst case
Example: inserting sorted array into BST creates skewed tree
Balancing ensures guaranteed O(log n) performance
